abstract: Copper-based catalysts are promising candidates for the electrochemical conversion of $\text{CO}_2$ into methane, a vital energy source. However, achieving high activity and selectivity remains a challenge due to a lack of fundamental design principles for navigating complex reaction pathways. In this work, we propose a dual-strategy design for carbon-supported Cu catalysts that integrates the atomic-scale tuning of active sites with the mesoscale engineering of the carbon support architecture to create an optimal local microenvironment for methane production. In situ X-ray absorption and Raman spectroscopy reveal that under reaction conditions, atomically dispersed $\text{Cu-N}_3$ sites undergo a dynamic reconstruction into nitrogen- and hydroxyl-immobilized $\text{Cu}_3$ ($\text{N,OH-Cu}_3$) clusters. These clusters exhibit a moderate adsorption affinity for *CO and a significantly lowered energy barrier for *CO hydrogenation, effectively favoring the $\text{CO}_2$-to-$\text{CH}_4$ pathway over thermodynamically demanding C-C coupling. Paradoxically, the choice of support is equally critical; we developed a block copolymer-derived carbon fiber featuring interconnected, bicontinuous mesochannels. This unique architecture creates an $\text{H}_2\text{O}$-deficient microenvironment and extends the residence time of CO intermediates, thereby suppressing the competing hydrogen evolution reaction and promoting deep reduction to methane. Consequently, this catalyst achieves an unprecedented Faradaic efficiency of 74.2% for methane at an industrially relevant current density of 300 mA $\text{cm}^{-2}$. This study provides a robust framework for steering reaction pathways in interfacial catalysis by coordinating atomic-level site engineering with mesoscale structural design.
